market_session: US regular session close; AP week-to-date basis

calculation.method: reported week-to-date point and percent changes from AP

text: For the week through the June 17 close, AP reported the S&P 500 down 11.36 points, or 0.2%; the Dow up 290.29 points, or 0.6%; the Nasdaq up 132.81 points, or 0.5%; and the Russell 2000 down 26.01 points, or 0.9%.

market_session: US regular session close; AP year-to-date basis

calculation.method: reported year-to-date point and percent changes from AP

text: For the year through the June 17 close, AP reported the S&P 500 up 574.60 points, or 8.4%; the Dow up 3,429.26 points, or 7.1%; the Nasdaq up 2,779.67 points, or 12.0%; and the Russell 2000 up 436.07 points, or 17.6%.
AP reported that Treasury yields climbed on rising expectations for a 2026 rate hike after the Fed projections.
The FOMC approved its June 17 statement by a 12-0 vote and maintained the federal funds target range at 3.50% to 3.75%.

The FOMC statement said economic activity was expanding at a solid pace, job gains had kept pace with the workforce, unemployment had changed little, and inflation remained elevated relative to the 2% goal in part because of supply shocks including energy.

calculation.method: median 2026 projections from the Federal Reserve accessible SEP table

text: The June SEP median 2026 projections were 2.2% real GDP growth, 4.3% unemployment, 3.6% PCE inflation, and 3.3% core PCE inflation.

calculation.method: counted June 2026 dots above, at, and below the current 3.625% midpoint of the 3.50%-3.75% target range

text: In the June SEP, 9 of 18 participants placed the 2026 year-end federal-funds-rate midpoint above the current 3.625% midpoint, 8 placed it at 3.625%, and 1 placed it below.

The June 17 implementation note maintained IORB at 3.65%, set standing overnight repo operations at 3.75%, set overnight reverse repos at 3.50% with a $160 billion per-counterparty daily limit, and kept the primary credit rate at 3.75%, effective June 18, 2026.

The pan-European STOXX Europe 600 rose about 0.5% (Yahoo Finance: +3.31 points, +0.52%, from the prior close of 636.00) to close at 639.31 on Wednesday, June 17, 2026, its fifth straight session of gains and a fresh record high after the sharp post-truce drop in oil prices.
The blue-chip Euro STOXX 50 rose 0.80% to 6,307.25 (prior close 6,257.42) on June 17, 2026, leaving the benchmark just below its June 2026 all-time high of 6,326.90 as the relief rally extended.

Germany's DAX 40 ended marginally higher at 24,934.67, up 0.10% from the prior close of 24,910.41, after a volatile session — its highest level in nearly two weeks and a fifth straight gain — as bank strength offset a sharp drop in automakers.
France's CAC 40 edged down 0.20% to close at 8,430.79 (prior close 8,447.27), the only major continental index to fall, as investors paused after recent gains and luxury heavyweights weighed.

The UK's FTSE 100 edged up 0.14% to 10,508.61 (prior close 10,494.21), a level corroborated by Yahoo Finance, as investors assessed steady UK inflation data and looked ahead to the Federal Reserve decision and Thursday's Bank of England meeting.

Autos were the worst-performing sector, down about 3.3% — their biggest one-day fall in nearly a month — after BMW lost roughly 8.3% (Trading Economics' earlier euro-area read cited a drop of more than 6%) on cutting its annual profit outlook, citing weakness in the Chinese market and the impact of the U.S.-Iran war; a German auto-supplier survey showed pessimists now outnumber optimists.

Heavyweight banks were the main support for the STOXX 600, advancing about 1.9% in their fifth straight session of gains — the longest streak since early January — as easing energy-driven inflation lifted the credit outlook; Commerzbank rose 4.9% and Deutsche Bank 2.3%, with Santander, UniCredit and Deutsche Bank each up around 2.5%.
Technology shares climbed about 1.5%, with chip-equipment names leading: Aixtron rose 6.7%, while BE Semiconductor and ASML each added roughly 4%.

On the FTSE 100, energy majors stayed under pressure as Shell and BP each fell more than 1%, with BAT down over 2%, Rio Tinto off more than 0.9% and Unilever down about 0.7%; HSBC gained about 1.5%, AstraZeneca 1.3% and GSK 1.1%. UK markets pared Bank of England rate-hike expectations after May inflation unexpectedly held steady at 2.8%, ahead of Thursday's BoE meeting.

Within the CAC 40, luxury led the losses with LVMH down 1.8% and Hermès off 1.3%, while STMicroelectronics slipped 0.8% after announcing a $1.5 billion convertible bond sale; financials and industrials advanced, with BNP Paribas up 1.2%, Crédit Agricole 1.6%, Schneider Electric 2.1% and Airbus 0.9%.

The dominant driver remained the U.S.-Iran peace agreement, expected to be signed Friday and to reopen the Strait of Hormuz; the sharp drop in oil prices since the announcement lifted the STOXX 600 to record highs and scaled back expectations that the ECB would need to raise rates this year, though the index still lags the U.S. S&P 500 and President Trump cautioned the deal was not final and could be reversed.

Trading stayed cautious into the European close ahead of the Federal Reserve's decision later in the day; rates were widely expected to be left unchanged, with investors focused on the communication style of new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh for clues on the policy path.

Dental-implant maker Straumann jumped 10.8% to top the STOXX 600 after sharply raising its 2026 profit outlook on stronger execution, rising China earnings and lower tariffs; used-car retailer Auto1 climbed 8.4% on long-term guidance, while Orange slipped 3.3% after Barclays reinstated coverage at 'equal weight'.
Barclays became the latest brokerage to close its underweight position on European equities, raising its STOXX Europe 600 price target to 670 points from 620.

text: ChosunBiz/News1 reported that large-cap semiconductor stocks led the KOSPI, SK hynix rose nearly 6%, individuals bought more than KRW 1T, institutions bought KRW 633.5B, and foreign investors sold more than KRW 1.5T.

market_session: Asia local market closes for June 17, 2026

calculation.method: compared reported daily percent changes for Nikkei 225, KOSPI, Shanghai Composite, and Hang Seng Index

text: Among the four tracked Asia closes on June 17, 2026, three indexes were positive and one was negative; KOSPI was the strongest at +1.58% and Hang Seng was the weakest at -0.74%.

The U.S. Treasury 2-year par yield jumped to 4.20% on June 17, 2026, up 15 basis points from 4.05% on June 16 and the largest single-day move along the June 2026 curve, after the FOMC's hawkish projections.
The U.S. Treasury 10-year par yield rose to 4.49% on June 17, 2026, up 6 basis points from 4.43% on June 16, per the official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve.
The U.S. Treasury 30-year par yield held at 4.93% on June 17, 2026, unchanged from June 16, as the hawkish repricing concentrated in shorter maturities rather than the long end.

The 2s10s Treasury par-yield spread flattened to +29 basis points on June 17, 2026, calculated as the 10-year par yield of 4.49% minus the 2-year par yield of 4.20%, a 9 basis point bear-flattening from +38 basis points on June 16 as the front end sold off harder than the long end.

The 2-year Treasury market yield climbed about 16-17 basis points to 4.216% on June 17, 2026, its highest level since February 2025, while the 10-year rose roughly 7 basis points to about 4.495%-4.499%, as the bond market repriced toward a 2026 rate hike with the heaviest selling in the final hour.
The Federal Reserve held its federal funds target range at 3.50%-3.75% on June 17, 2026, in a unanimous 12-0 vote with no dovish dissenters, at Kevin Warsh's first meeting as Chair.

The June SEP lifted the median year-end 2026 federal funds rate projection to 3.8% from 3.4% in March, with nine officials now anticipating at least one rate hike by end-2026; new Chair Warsh was the only one of 19 officials who did not submit a rate-path projection.

The June 17 FOMC statement removed prior language that had flagged the likelihood of rate cuts in 2026, a hawkish shift, even as it described economic activity as expanding at a solid pace despite elevated uncertainty from the Middle East conflict.

After the decision, fed funds futures priced roughly a 72% chance of a rate hike by October and cut the probability of rates simply holding through year-end to about 13%, down from 40% on Tuesday, with a September hike seen as more likely than a hold.
The S&P 500 fell 91.25 points, or 1.21%, to close at 7,420.10 on June 17, 2026, selling off sharply in the final hour after the Fed's hawkish projections.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 507.12 points, or 0.98%, to 51,492.55 on June 17, 2026, swinging from a morning gain of about 0.5% to a 1% loss and snapping a two-session streak of record closes.
The Nasdaq Composite fell 354.69 points, or 1.34%, to 26,021.66 on June 17, 2026, the weakest of the major indexes as higher-rate fears pressured growth and technology names.

Breadth was broadly negative on June 17: all 11 S&P 500 sectors closed lower, led down by communication services (about -3%) with industrials most resilient (-0.1%); the small-cap Russell 2000 fell 0.7% to 2,917.98 and the Cboe Volatility Index rose 2 points to 18.44.
The U.S. dollar index rose 0.9% to 100.47 on June 17, 2026, as the hawkish Fed shift and higher Treasury yields drew flows into the dollar.

WTI crude was about $76.10 a barrel on June 17, 2026, essentially flat (+0.06%) and edging up after President Trump said the U.S.-Iran agreement was not final and the war could resume, having slid earlier in the week on the preliminary peace deal.

U.S. retail sales rose 0.9% in May 2026, well above the 0.5% consensus and up 6.9% year over year, the fourth straight strong month, with service-station receipts up 3.4% on higher gasoline prices, underscoring the resilient demand and sticky-inflation backdrop behind the Fed's hawkish turn.

Risk sentiment turned defensive on June 17, 2026: the S&P 500 erased an early gain and fell 91.25 points, or 1.21%, to close at 7,420.10, losing steam into the close after Fed Chair Kevin Warsh's first press conference, a session-wide reversal rather than a single-sector wobble.

Growth and momentum names bore the brunt of the de-risking: the Nasdaq Composite sank 354.69 points, or 1.34%, to 26,021.66, the weakest major index, with heavy mega-cap weights including SpaceX (-4.9%), Microsoft (-3.8%), Amazon (-3.5%) and Nvidia (-1.3%) dragging on the tape.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average swung from a morning gain of about 280 points to a 507.12-point loss, closing down 0.98% at 51,492.55, underscoring that the late-day risk-off reached beyond growth stocks into blue chips.
Breadth was uniformly negative: all 11 S&P 500 industry sectors turned red after the Fed meeting, confirming a broad de-risking rather than an isolated, single-sector pullback.

The Cboe Volatility Index jumped 2.03 points, or 12.37%, to close at 18.44 on June 17, 2026, yet remained at a historically moderate level, signaling a controlled hedging bid into the Fed decision rather than outright panic.

Small caps fell less than large caps, with the Russell 2000 down 0.72% to 2,917.98 versus the S&P 500's 1.21% drop, indicating the damage was concentrated in crowded mega-cap growth rather than a broad, small-cap-led risk-off.

Positioning repriced hard toward tightening after the meeting: CME Group data showed the probability of at least one 2026 rate hike jumping to about 84% from 59.5% a day earlier, while the odds of the Fed simply holding through year-end collapsed to roughly 13% from 40% on Tuesday, with a September hike now seen as more likely than a hold.

subject.name: FOMC sentiment driver
subject.event: June 2026 FOMC decision and Warsh debut

text: The mood shift had two reinforcing drivers: a hawkish policy signal - the statement removed prior language flagging likely 2026 rate cuts and Warsh stressed the Fed would deliver on price stability, which Rosenblatt's Michael James called a clearly hawkish tilt - and uncertainty over Warsh's announced new chapter for the Fed, including ending forward guidance and a wide-ranging policy review, which several strategists said added communication risk and helped stocks lose steam after his press conference.
